data.title: Cases of drug-resistant salmonella, several among kids, linked to outbreak; CDC issues warning
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention is warning of more than two dozen cases of possibly drug-resistant salmonella cases across 13 states.

In an update last week, the CDC said all 34 people — several of whom are children— have the same strain of salmonella, which has been linked to backyard poultry.

To make matters more difficult, the CDC says an analysis of all 34 patients showed the strain may be resistant to the antibiotic fosfomycin. Samples from eight of those patients showed there could be resistance to other antibiotics commonly used in salmonella infections.

Here’s what to know.

Where have the drug-resistant cases of salmonella been found?

So far, cases have primarily been found in the Midwest. As of Tuesday, the CDC shows Michigan has reported the most confirmed cases at six, followed by Ohio and Wisconsin, which each have had five cases.

Indiana, Kentucky, and Maine have each had three confirmed cases, while Maryland and West Virginia have recorded two each.

Florida, Illinois, Mississippi, New Hampshire, and Tennessee have each confirmed a single case.

What do we know about the patients?

The patients range in age from less than a year old to 78 years old, with the median age being 12 years old.

According to the CDC, roughly a dozen of those who became ill are under the age of 5.

Thirteen of those who fell ill required hospitalization. The CDC warns, though, that there could be more sick people in the same outbreak. Cases have so far been reported between late February and the end of March.

Health officials interviewed 29 patients, with 23 saying they had contact with backyard poultry in the week before becoming ill.

Fourteen people said they own backyard birds, with all but one saying they got new poultry since the start of the year. The CDC said investigators are working to determine if there is a common source of the poultry.

What to know about the salmonella infections

While the CDC has linked the above cases to backyard poultry, it’s not clear which species in particular is to blame. The agency notes that any backyard birds, including ducks and chickens, can carry the bacteria.

You don’t need to touch the poultry to be exposed, either. Touching anything in their environment, then touching your mouth or food without washing your hands, can expose you to the salmonella germs.

Salmonella infections are commonly associated with diarrhea, fever and stomach cramps, according to the CDC. Symptoms can begin between six hours and six days after you’ve ingested the bacteria. Most people are able to recover without receiving treatment within four to seven days.

Illnesses may be more severe for young children, the elderly, and those with weakened immune systems. Some may require medical treatment or hospitalization, the CDC explains.

Infections can only be diagnosed with a laboratory test of a person’s stool, body tissue or fluids.

To protect yourself, the CDC encourages washing your hands after touching birds, anything in their habitat, and their eggs. Don’t wear the shoes or boots you use in the birds’ area inside your home, and keep the poultry and their supplies outside.

Children younger than 5 should not handle the birds or their materials, as they are more likely to fall ill from salmonella, the CDC adds.

Every year, salmonella causes roughly 1.35 million illnesses and 420 deaths.

data.title: We need AI, but the political backlash cannot be ignored
data.description:
Artificial Intelligence holds the promise to improve life on the planet almost beyond description, such as developing new cancer therapies, but breakneck development of the data centers that run AI (along with more mundane applications from gambling to porn to cat videos) has spurred a grass-roots political backlash that is growing and could be a major factor in this year’s elections.

If dismissed by political and corporate leaders, the backlash threatens to result in extreme measures such as the ban on data center development just passed by the legislature of Maine, which Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.) and Rep. Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez (D-N.Y.) want to impose nationally.

Local rejection of data center projects is growing rapidly. Virginia, the “Data Center Alley” of concentrated development, reflects the magnitude of the backlash. A recent Washington Post poll showed that Virginians’ support for data centers locating in their communities dropped from 69 percent just three years ago to only 35 percent now. The precipitous drop in support for data centers cuts across all voters, Democratic, Republican and independent, and in all areas of the state, urban, suburban and rural.

The backlash on the Bernie-AOC left may be rooted in ideological rage against corporate wealth and power, epitomized by Big Tech companies which are now the richest and most powerful in history. But hostility is growing on the right as well, reflected in Florida Republican Gov. Ron DeSantis’s (R) proposals to restrict data centers.

Much of the growing opposition comes from a realization that the rapid development of data centers, especially those running AI, has pushed the power grid to the precipice of a real crisis of reliability. Data centers use huge amounts of power, and the unprecedented growth in power demand has far exceeded the ability of the power grid to add new power supply resources quickly enough to prevent power shortfalls.

And data centers typically require new wires and generation, both of which are extremely costly. As consumers’ power bills rise relentlessly and politicians respond to voter ire over their bills, data centers have become a convenient villain.

Further fueling the backlash are politically tone-deaf proposals such as the Trump administration’s directive to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission to take an unprecedented action: claiming authority to override the states over how and when data centers connect to the power grid. The purposes of this proposal, being pushed by the industry, are obvious: for federal regulators to mandate radically shorter deadlines on utilities to connect data centers to the grid, and potentially to block state measures meant to ensure that data centers pay the full costs of their grid connections and wires usage. The proposal is bad policy and even worse politics.

We all live life online now, so we are all building data centers. We all stand to benefit from the promise of AI. But there are real costs that must be honestly calculated and fully allocated to the cost causers. Soundbites about “winning the AI race with China” will not assuage voters angry about skyrocketing power bills. Indeed, to the extent AI is essential to national security, defense-related AI should be funded the same way the U.S. has always funded advanced military technologies, with tax dollars, not through power bills paid by consumers.

The tech bros’ mantra of “move fast and break things” may work in Silicon Valley, but not in the power grid. Electric utilities and their regulators have an obligation to serve the entire public and the entire economy, not just one industry, no matter how promising. And in a volatile election year, being on the wrong side of a growing grass-roots backlash is dangerous.

Mark Christie directs the Center for Energy Law and Policy at William and Mary Law School, where he teaches energy law. He is a former Chairman of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission and served 17 years as a utility regulator in Virginia.

data.title: Chilling bodycam video shows California gunfight that killed officer, innocent father
data.description:
(KTLA) — Dramatic body-worn camera footage of a deadly shootout last year in Baldwin Park, Calif., shows the moment a police officer was killed and another wounded in an incident that also left an innocent bystander dead.

The deadly encounter began around 7:15 p.m. on May 31, in a neighborhood where police responded to reports of a man firing a rifle in the street.

An arriving Baldwin Park Police Department officer who was first on scene saw an unresponsive person lying on the ground. Before he could help, however, he immediately began taking fire from an assault-style rifle wielded by the suspect, identified as 22-year-old Eduardo Robert Medina-Berumen.

The suspect also fired at a second patrol unit that arrived moments later, hitting the vehicle twice. One of the shots hit officer Anthony Pimentel’s windshield, sending glass and shrapnel into his face.

Surveillance and witness video showed neighbors, many of whom had gathered for weekend parties and get-togethers, scrambling for safety as rapid gunfire echoed through the neighborhood.

A law enforcement helicopter helped ground units find Medina-Berumen lying in the front yard of a nearby home.

Deputies with the Los Angeles County Sheriff’s Department arrived along with additional Baldwin Park police officers, including 35-year-old Samuel Riveros, a nine-year veteran of the department, who began a dangerous approach toward the suspect.

Riveros was shot while across the street from the shooter’s position.

A team of officers rushed to his aid and dragged him out of the line of fire. He was airlifted to USC Medical Center, where he later died from his injuries.

Officer Pimentel was hospitalized in stable condition and was expected to recover.

The bystander, 43-year-old Darius Wong, a father of two young daughters, was shot in the back. He was also rushed to the hospital, where he was pronounced dead.

Using an armored vehicle, police and deputies positioned themselves in front of the yard where Medina-Berumen had taken cover and shot him when he allegedly reached for the rifle he had been using.

He was transported to the hospital in stable condition and later charged by the Los Angeles County District Attorney’s Office with two counts of murder, one count of attempted murder and possession of an assault weapon.

If convicted, he faces the death penalty or life in prison without the possibility of parole.

“Sam was a dedicated officer who represented our department with dignity and honor,” Baldwin Park Police Chief Robert A. López said of Riveros. “In the face of danger, Sam selflessly put the safety of others before his own and gave his life doing so. We will never forget Sam, and we will lean on one another in our shared grief as we move forward.”

Footage of the deadly gunfight was released by the Baldwin Park Police Department.

data.title: Maurene Comey's lawsuit over firing by Trump administration may proceed, judge rules
data.description:
Maurene Comey, a former federal prosecutor and the daughter of former FBI Director James Comey, may proceed with her lawsuit alleging the Trump administration fired her for political reasons, a judge ruled Tuesday.

The ruling from U.S. District Judge Jesse Furman came over the Justice Department’s objection, and found that Maurene Comey’s lawsuit belongs in federal court instead of before an executive branch agency that presides over federal worker complaints.

The judge said her case “falls outside the universe” of cases Congress meant for the Merit Systems Protection Board (MSPB) to resolve.

“Maurene Comey was, by all accounts, an exemplary Assistant United States Attorney,” Furman wrote in a 27-page ruling, pointing to her accolades in contrast with President Trump’s disdain for her father.

“She was given one and only one reason for her removal: Article II of the U.S. Constitution, which ‘vest[s]’ the ‘executive Power’ in the President,” he said.

Maurene Comey sued the Trump administration in September over her July dismissal from her role as a prosecutor in the Southern District of New York (SDNY). She alleged that her firing came “solely or substantially” because of Trump’s long-standing feud with her father.

Trump fired James Comey as FBI director in 2017. It became a focal point of special counsel Robert Mueller’s investigation into whether Trump sought to obstruct the probe into Russian interference in the 2016 presidential election. The former FBI director also became a vocal critic of Trump.

The Justice Department hit James Comey last year with false statements and obstruction charges stemming from 2020 testimony he gave Congress concerning leaks at the FBI, but those charges were dismissed over the unlawful appointment of the prosecutor who pursued them. The Trump administration has appealed.

When Maurene Comey was fired, she wrote in a letter to her colleagues that “fear is the tool of a tyrant, wielded to suppress independent thought.” 

She worked in the U.S. attorney’s office for nearly a decade prosecuting high-profile criminal cases, including against Sean “Diddy” Combs and, earlier, Jeffrey Epstein and Ghislaine Maxwell.

Furman, the judge, set a May 28 hearing for an initial pretrial conference. He ordered the Trump administration to respond to her claims within two weeks.

data.title: DHS says more than 1,000 TSA officers 'forced to leave' due to shutdown
data.description:
More than 1,000 Transportation Security Administration (TSA) officers have departed amid the ongoing shutdown of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), the department said this week.

DHS wrote Monday on the social platform X that more than 1,000 TSA officers “have been forced to leave the force” since the shutdown began in mid-February.

“Ahead of the FIFA World Cup and summer travel, this loss has SIGNIFICANTLY decreased TSA’s ability to meet passenger demand and left critical gaps in staffing, as each new recruit requires 4-6 MONTHS of training,” DHS added.

The U.S., along with Canada and Mexico, is hosting the men’s World Cup starting in June. Earlier this month, the U.S. Travel Association warned that while the event could “deliver major economic gains” for the country, safety has “emerged as a top concern among potential visitors.”

President Trump earlier this month authorized the release of nearly $10 billion in emergency funds from the GOP’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act to pay DHS employees, housed under 22 separate agencies. 

But Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin said last week that the funding will run out in early May, leaving his department unable to pay employee salaries. As of April 17, the emergency funds had been depleted to less than $1.4 billion, according to the Office of Management and Budget.

Until the Trump administration tapped into those funds, TSA officer absences resulted in lengthy wait times at airports across the country.

Acting TSA Administrator Ha Nguyen McNeill told the House Homeland Security Committee on March 25 that as of the prior day, roughly 460 officers had left the force since the current funding lapse began. She also said that roughly 1,110 TSA officers departed during the 43-day shutdown in the fall. 

“TSA does not have the luxury of time,” McNeill warned at the time. “The FIFA World Cup is kicking off on June 11 — less than three months away. We are anticipating a significant influx in passenger volume as fans travel through our airports to see the games.

“Even if TSA were to hire new officers upon conclusion of the DHS shutdown, those officers would not be able to work on the checkpoint until well after the World Cup has concluded.”

Republicans on Capitol Hill, meanwhile, are trying to fund DHS via a two-step process — first through a bill to fund all agencies except for Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol, and then through the budget reconciliation process to fund the aforementioned agencies.

The shutdown of DHS stemmed from Democrats demanding reforms to immigration enforcement procedures in the wake of federal officers fatally shooting two U.S. citizens, Renee Good and Alex Pretti, in Minneapolis earlier this year.

Speaker Mike Johnson said Monday that a bipartisan Senate bill to fund most of DHS has “some problematic language.” The Louisiana Republican added that his conference has drafted a “modified” version of the bill.

data.title: Trump allies fume over Obama's post on shooting suspect's motives
data.description:
Allies of President Trump are upset over a post by former President Obama about the motives of the suspected shooter at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.

On Sunday, Obama posted on the social media platform X that “although we don’t yet have the details about the motives behind last night’s shooting at the White House Correspondents Dinner, it’s incumbent upon all us to reject the idea that violence has any place in our democracy.”

“It’s also a sobering reminder of the courage and sacrifice that U.S. Secret Service Agents show every day. I’m grateful to them – and thankful that the agent who was shot is going to be okay,” the former president added.

In response to Obama’s post, conservative influencer Benny Johnson responded by listing off alleged pieces of evidence around the suspect targeting Trump and contrasting that with the former president saying there weren’t yet details about the motives behind the shooting.

The suspect in Saturday’s shooting has been identified as 31-year-old Cole Allen, a teacher and game developer that law enforcement has said traveled from California to Washington, D.C., to disrupt the dinner.

In a manifesto first reported by the New York Post, Allen calls himself a “Friendly Federal Assassin.” 

Allen allegedly wrote that members of the Trump administration were “targets, prioritized from highest-ranking to lowest,” with guests at the dinner and employees being “not targets at all.” He also said he would “still go through most everyone here to get to the targets if it were absolutely necessary,”

Sen. Mike Lee (R-Utah) responded to the former president’s post by saying “it was politically motivated.”

“He made that pretty clear,” Lee added on X, referring to the suspect.

Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Administrator Lee Zeldin on X told Obama to “not pretend to be this clueless about motive.”

“The attempted assassin put out an anti-Trump manifesto about wanting to kill Trump Admin officials, minutes before trying to storm a ballroom filled with the President, VP, Cabinet, and many others from his Admin,” he added.

The Hill has reached out to Obama’s office for comment.

data.title: Appeals court rejects Trump administration mandatory detention policy
data.description:
A federal appeals court rejected the Trump administration’s policy of detaining almost all those it is seeking to deport, determining they must be given the chance to seek bond amid their pursuit to remain in the country.

Figures of those being held in detention have swollen to historic levels under the Trump administration, with the government detaining those with no criminal record and who have already resided in the U.S. for years.

Judge Joseph Bianco, a Trump appointee, wrote that even if the administration’s reading of immigration detention statues “were plausible—and it is not,” the court would still have constitutional concerns over “what would be the broadest mass-detention-without bond mandate in our Nation’s history for millions of noncitizens.”

The 3-0 ruling from the Second Circuit brings together a Clinton and Biden appointee alongside a jurist appointed by President Trump. But the decision sets up a circuit court split with rulings from the fifth and eighth circuits.

In the latest ruling, however, Bianco notes that “over ninety percent of district court judges” have sided with migrants who have filed habeas corpus petitions seeking their release from immigration detention.

The ruling questions the disparity between immigration laws that call for the detention of migrants with consistent actions of Congress in funding detention space for just a fraction of those who might be held during deportation proceedings.

“The government offers no explanation for why the same Congress that was so concerned with the detention of 100,000 people…would have turned a blind eye to the consequences of mandating detention” for a group that could be as large as 2 million people, Bianco wrote.

“Moreover, it is even harder to explain why the government, if tasked with detaining the huge number of noncitizens covered by Section 1225, would have neglected for almost thirty years to increase its detention capacity enough to actually implement that mandate,” he added, referencing a section of U.S. immigration law.

DEVELOPING

data.title: Aiding the abduction of Ukrainian children must come with consequences
data.description:
“They do not like the light of day. They’re like vampires,” former U.S. War Crimes Ambassador David Crane said about private corporations and their leaders that facilitate war crimes.  

At a hearing of the Tom Lantos Human Rights Commission in the U.S. Capitol last month, witnesses and members of Congress considered that Russian President Vladimir Putin is unlikely to face the consequences from international legal bodies for crimes against humanity, including Russia’s abduction of Ukrainian children. 

But, as Crane and other witnesses pointed out, it’s time for lower-level government officials and executives at Russian companies that aid and abet child abductions to face consequences from the United States and other opposing countries.  

The hearing came on the same day that Yale School of Public Health’s Humanitarian Research Lab released its latest study, finding that Russian energy giants Rosneft and Gazprom contributed to the deportation, indoctrination and militarization of Ukrainian children.  

According to the report, the two companies, along with their subsidiaries and trade unions, “underwrote and facilitated the transport” of approximately 2,158 children from Russian-occupied Ukrainian territory to at least six summer camps inside Russia and Russian-occupied Ukraine, three of which were owned by Gazprom subsidiaries.

Russia’s oil and gas sector is projected to account for over 22 percent of Russia’s federal budget in 2026 and is essential to sustaining Russia’s ongoing invasion. Rosneft and Gazprom are the sector’s largest companies, led by some of Putin’s closest allies.  

The allegations implicate the two companies in Russia’s well-documented crime of abducting Ukrainian children. International law considers the transfer of children from an occupied territory to the territory of the occupying power to be a war crime, regardless of motive.  

Ukraine estimates that nearly 20,000 Ukrainian children have been abducted. Throughout the Russian-occupied territories, Ukrainian schoolchildren are subjected to pro-Russian “patriotic education initiatives” — part of Putin’s goal of erasing Ukrainian identity through “Russification.” 

In 2023, the International Criminal Court issued arrest warrants for Putin and Russia’s Commissioner for Children’s Rights Maria Lvova-Belova, holding them “allegedly responsible for the war crime of unlawful deportation of population (children)” and the “unlawful transfer of population (children) from occupied areas of Ukraine to the Russian Federation.”   

But although Article 27 of the Rome Statute establishing the court does not exempt heads of state from prosecution, Russia is not a signatory to the statute. Moscow obviously has no intention of handing over Putin, Lvova-Belova or any other Russian official. 

Another approach is needed to pressure those individuals and entities involved in trafficking Ukrainian children into Russia.

Out of 44 entities accused in Yale’s report that facilitated the transfer of children, a shocking 80 percent have not been sanctioned by the United States or Europe. The report listed camps, oil and gas subsidiaries, trade unions and high-level company directors.  

“Corporations are a lurking shadow behind atrocities,” Crane said, drawing on his experience prosecuting war crimes in West Africa, adding, “there’s nothing wrong with naming and shaming. It’s bad press for any corporation to be named in something like this.”  

In addition to naming and shaming, the U.S. and European governments should initiate a campaign to target senior executives one or two levels below the billionaire oligarchs, Russians who have operational control of subsidiary businesses or organizations involved in Russia’s crimes against Ukrainian children.

This cohort may not have yachts like the oligarchs, but they retain access to the West. In 2025, 49 percent of Russian foreign real estate transactions were in Europe. Russians are obtaining residence permits, the “ability to travel within the Schengen Area, open accounts in the [European Union], and manage financial resources,” according to data from the international real estate consulting firm, Intermark Global.

The U.S. saw a 78.5 percent year-on-year surge in Russian demand for real estate in the first half of 2025. Travel to the West, especially Western Europe, has also rebounded since the early days of the war.

The U.S. Treasury and State departments must target Russia’s executive class, including those listed in Yale’s report, with targeted sanctions, asset freezes and travel bans for facilitating child abductions to disincentivize their participation in the scheme.

Congress and the administration should also look to identify persons and entities engaged in this human trafficking effort and sanction them under the Global Magnitsky Act, which allows for asset freezes and visa bans for those found to be involved in human rights abuses and corruption.

Creating consequences for their participation in Russia’s atrocities may do more to curtail their actions than targeting Russia’s leadership and flagship corporations.

Dmitriy Shapiro is a research analyst and editor at the Foundation for Defense of Democracies.

data.title: Are Democrats winning elections but losing the narrative? Join the live discussion
data.description:
Democrats are winning races — and even redistricting battles — but some in the party worry they’re not yet winning the argument.

Join The Hill’s Amie Parnes and Editor in Chief Ian Swanson for a discussion Tuesday afternoon on Democrats’ messaging and political fallout from the shooting Saturday night at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.

The analysis will include a Q&A session on the latest news in the early race for the White House.

The event is scheduled to begin at noon EDT.

Register and watch the live discussion above.

If you register before the event is live, you will receive an email containing the link to join once it starts. If you register as the event is starting, you will be transferred directly to the livestream.

data.title: McCabe: 'Fascinating' WHCA dinner shooting suspect 'made no effort whatsoever' to cover tracks
data.description:
Former acting FBI Director Andrew McCabe on Tuesday said it was “fascinating” to him that the suspect accused of targeting Trump administration officials at the White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA) dinner over the weekend appeared to make “no effort whatsoever to cover his tracks.”  

Cole Allen, 31, was charged with attempting to assassinate President Trump and other firearm-related offenses after allegedly opening fire at the Washington Hilton hotel on Saturday.  

“He left an extensive record, certainly in the social media posts that you referred to earlier, all of which will be helpful to the prosecution in their effort to prove his intent to try to assassinate the president,” McCabe said during an interview on CNN.

“He wrote an extensive letter or manifesto or whatever you want to call it, and sent it to many people, some of whom handed it directly to law enforcement, in which he basically confessed to the crime ahead of time, explained why he was doing it, who he was targeting in what order,” he continued.

Allen was allegedly targeting White House officials “prioritized from highest-ranking to lowest,” according to a manifesto confirmed to be written by the suspect.

A White House official also told The Hill that Allen’s sister informed authorities that her brother often made “radical statements” and referenced plans to do “something” in response to the issues facing today’s world.   

Allen’s brother alerted police in New London, Conn., to the alleged manifesto just minutes before the incident occurred at the WHCA dinner on Saturday, according to the official.

Authorities said Allen was armed with a shotgun, handgun and multiple knives when he rushed the hotel where Trump, the first lady and several Cabinet officials were gathered for the annual black-tie affair with journalists.

A Secret Service agent was struck but saved by a bulletproof vest, Trump told reporters after the incident. No one inside was hurt, but the chaos sent attendees ducking for cover under tables as shots rang out.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said on Sunday that the suspect traveled from Los Angeles to Washington, D.C. with plans to interrupt the dinner.

McCabe argued on Tuesday that the evidence in the case was “already quite strong,” but authorities would continue working to gather information to help better understand Allen’s motive.

“What investigators are looking for now is additional intelligence that can tell us a little bit more about why this young man went in the direction that he did,” he said. “And that‘s an important question for our intelligence and law enforcement folks to understand.”

Allen, a professional game designer with a master’s degree in computer science, made his initial appearance in federal court on Monday. He did not enter a plea.
